title: US stocks are off to their worst start versus the global market since 1995
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: US stocks had their worst start in 2026 compared to global markets since 1995. This trend is according to Goldman Sachs data.
excerpt: US stocks had their worst start in 2026 compared to global markets since 1995. This trend is according to Goldman Sachs data.

content.introduction: US equities have commenced 2026 with a performance disparity against global markets not witnessed since 1995, marking a significant shift in the investment landscape. According to Goldman Sachs research data, this underperformance reflects broader concerns about US market valuations and potential economic headwinds that may be weighing on investor sentiment.
body:
## Historical Context and Market Performance

The current market dynamic represents a notable reversal from the long-standing dominance of US equities in the global arena. Goldman Sachs analysis indicates that the year-to-date performance gap between US stocks and their international counterparts is the widest in 31 years, suggesting a potential inflection point in global market leadership. This divergence has been particularly pronounced in developed markets outside the US, with European and Japanese indices outperforming their American counterparts by significant margins. The last time such a stark contrast was observed was during the mid-1990s, prior to the technology boom that propelled US markets to unprecedented heights.

## Factors Driving the Divergence

Several interconnected factors have contributed to this underperformance by US equities relative to global markets. First, concerns about stretched valuations in US markets have become more pronounced, with price-to-earnings ratios trading at significant premiums to international markets. Second, the Federal Reserve's monetary policy stance, while potentially nearing its peak, continues to exert upward pressure on borrowing costs, creating headwinds for growth-sensitive sectors. Additionally, international markets have benefited from more accommodative central bank policies in regions like Europe and Japan, which have provided a tailwind for local equity performance. The strength of the US dollar has also played a role, as American multinationals face challenges when converting foreign earnings back into dollars.

## Sectoral Analysis and Regional Performance

The performance divergence extends across various sectors and geographic regions. Technology, which has been the primary driver of US market gains in recent years, has faced increased headwinds from rising interest rates and regulatory scrutiny. Meanwhile, cyclical and value-oriented sectors in international markets have shown greater resilience, particularly in regions with stronger commodity exports and more accommodative monetary policies. European markets, for instance, have benefited from reduced energy price volatility and more stable economic growth prospects. Emerging markets, while still facing structural challenges, have shown pockets of strength, particularly in Asia, where manufacturing and export-oriented economies have capitalized on shifting global supply chains.

content.outlook:
Looking ahead, A survey of 204 investment trust investors found 29% of respondents believe the US will fare worst, followed by 26% who said property would be worst performing.

However, despite fears of a ‘tech bubble’ in the US, technology is still expected to be the best performing sector over the coming year, a. Market participants will continue to monitor developments closely for signals about the direction of global markets trends and their broader implications for portfolio allocation and risk management strategies.
